title: Sector-specific processing rules

compliance.assurance_rationale: NO Personopplysningsloven §8 — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NO Personopplysningsloven §8 (Processing of special categories of personal data) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.26.19: Sector-specific processing rules.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.26.19' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621) (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=emr sourcetype IN ("epic:access","cerner:audit") earliest=-24h
| lookup clinical_shift_roster.csv user OUTPUT shift_start, shift_end, ward
| eval on_shift=if(_time>=shift_start AND _time<=shift_end,1,0)
| where on_shift=0 AND action="READ" AND patient_unit!=ward
| stats count by user, patient_unit, ward
description: Maps processing activities to sector codes via lookup to evidence sector-specific obligations.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NO Personopplysningsloven compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes emr and sourcetypes cerner:audit, epic:access. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders. Indexes required: `index=emr`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=cerner:audit`, `sourcetype=epic:access`.
visualization: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Authentication (CIM data model).

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Epic Audit Log Integration (facility-specific TA) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Epic Audit Log Integration (facility-specific TA)" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `emr` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `clinical_shift_roster.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('clinical_shift_roster.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§8`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NO Personopplysningsloven §8 (Processing of special categories of personal data) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.26.19: Sector-specific processing rules." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Epic Audit Log Integration (facility-specific TA) , landing events in `emr` under `epic:access`, `cerner:audit`. Epic Hyperspace audit exports run 5000-50000 events/day depending on facility size; at 1 KB/event plan 5-50 GB/month per facility.

Configure the input through Universal Forwarder on the Epic audit export host; coordinate export schedule with Epic admin team. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[monitor:///opt/epic/audit/export/*.json]
index = epic
sourcetype = epic:audit
disabled = 0
crcSalt = <SOURCE>
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `USER_ID, PAT_ID, AccessType, AccessContext, WorkstationName, Department, _time`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=emr earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 3:** Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 4:** Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Sector-specific processing rules," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NO Personopplysningsloven control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `clinical_shift_roster.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`shift_start, shift_end, ward`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `user`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `clinical_shift_roster.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval user = lower(user)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 24 hours.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.26.19: Sector-specific processing rules]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.26.19,reg=NO_Personopplysningsloven
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=emr sourcetype=epic:access earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `on_shift`, `patient_unit`, `user`, `ward`. Run:

```spl
index=emr sourcetype=epic:access earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table on_shift, patient_unit, user, ward
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `clinical_shift_roster.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup clinical_shift_roster.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.26.19: NO Personopplysningsloven evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Distribution (`events by host`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (`top users`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`policy violations`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'NO Personopplysningsloven current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NO Personopplysningsloven-specific failure modes:**

• Datatilsynet (DPA) coordination: Norwegian DPA. Detections on personal data obligations route to Datatilsynet, not EDPB.

• GDPR supplementation: Personopplysningsloven supplements GDPR in Norway. Detections must track both — Norway-specific rules (e.g. fødselsnummer handling) are additional.

• Fødselsnummer (national ID) handling: special rules apply to national identifier processing. Detections on PII must differentiate fødselsnummer from other IDs.

• Employee monitoring: Norway has strict employee monitoring rules. Detections on user activity monitoring must consider the employment-law overlay.
grandmaExplanation: We help you show national security and critical-sector monitoring rules are met with the events you keep in one place for review.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Sector-specific processing rules' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper NO Personopplysningsloven control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the NO Personopplysningsloven control objective.

evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with NO Personopplysningsloven control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the NO Personopplysningsloven programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
